knowledge.what_it_is: Software architecture refers to the high-level design and structure of an application or system, encompassing both technical and business considerations. It involves defining the overall framework, components, interactions, and interfaces that enable a software system to meet its functional and non-functional requirements.
what_you_will_learn: System architecture patterns — understanding Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA), Microservices Architecture, Event-Driven Architecture, and Monolithic Architecture
Architecture styles — learning about Layered Architecture, Model-View-Controller (MVC) Architecture, Model-View-Presenter (MVP) Architecture, and Model-View-ViewModel (MVVM) Architecture

Design principles — applying SOLID principles (Single responsibility, Open/closed, Liskov substitution, Interface segregation, Dependency inversion), Don't Repeat Yourself (DRY), and You Ain't Gonna Need It (YAGNI)
Component-based architecture — designing systems using reusable components with well-defined interfaces and interactions
Service-oriented architecture — defining services as self-contained units of functionality with standardized interfaces and APIs
Microservices architecture — breaking down monolithic applications into smaller, independent services communicating through APIs and message queues
Event-driven architecture — designing systems around events and asynchronous communication using event buses and message queues

knowledge.what_it_is: A software architect is a technical leader responsible for designing, building, and maintaining the overall architecture of an organization's software systems. They ensure that the system meets the business requirements, is scalable, secure, and efficient.
what_you_will_learn: System design principles — understanding trade-offs between scalability, performance, security, and maintainability
Architecture patterns — applying microservices, event-driven, service-oriented, and layered architectures to complex systems
Cloud computing fundamentals — designing for cloud-native applications on AWS, Azure, Google Cloud, or Kubernetes
Containerization with Docker — packaging, deploying, and managing containerized applications
Service discovery and registry with etcd, Consul, or ZooKeeper — enabling service registration, lookup, and communication
API design principles — designing RESTful APIs, GraphQL schemas, and gRPC services for efficient data exchange
Security considerations — implementing authentication, authorization, encryption, and access control in software systems

knowledge.what_it_is: Architecture levels refer to the hierarchical organization of software systems, breaking down complex systems into smaller, manageable components. This approach helps ensure scalability, maintainability, and reusability.
what_you_will_learn: Monolithic architecture — a single, self-contained unit with all layers integrated, ideal for small projects or proof-of-concepts
Microservices architecture — a collection of small, independent services communicating through APIs, suitable for large-scale systems with high scalability needs
Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA) — a design pattern where services are loosely coupled and communicate via standardized interfaces, promoting reuse and flexibility
Event-Driven Architecture (EDA) — a pattern where applications react to events generated by other components or external sources, enabling real-time processing and asynchronous communication
Layered architecture — a structure consisting of distinct layers, each responsible for a specific function, such as presentation, application, business logic, data access, and storage
Service Mesh architecture — an extension of microservices, using a dedicated infrastructure layer to manage service discovery, traffic management, and security

Cloud-Native architecture — designed specifically for cloud environments, emphasizing scalability, elasticity, and resilience through containerization (e.g., Docker) and orchestration (e.g., Kubernetes)
Event-Driven Microservices (EDM) — a hybrid approach combining EDA with microservices, enabling real-time processing and asynchronous communication in large-scale systems

knowledge.what_it_is: Application architecture refers to the high-level design and structure of an application's components, including its technical infrastructure, data storage, and interfaces. As a software architect, you will be responsible for designing and implementing the overall architecture of an application, ensuring it meets the requirements of scalability, maintainability, and performance.
what_you_will_learn: Microservices architecture — designing applications as a collection of small, independent services that communicate with each other using APIs
Service-oriented architecture (SOA) — structuring applications around services that provide specific business capabilities
Event-driven architecture (EDA) — designing applications to respond to events and notifications from other systems or components
Containerization with Docker — packaging and shipping applications in containers for easier deployment and management
Kubernetes orchestration — automating the deployment, scaling, and management of containerized applications
API-first design — designing APIs as the primary interface for interacting with an application's services and data
Domain-driven design (DDD) — structuring applications around business domains and entities to improve maintainability and scalability

knowledge.what_it_is: Solution architecture refers to the high-level design and planning of software systems, encompassing technical, business, and organizational considerations. As a Software Architect, you will be responsible for creating solution architectures that meet business needs while ensuring scalability, maintainability, and reliability.
what_you_will_learn: Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA) — designing systems as collections of services that communicate with each other using standardized interfaces
Microservices Architecture — breaking down monolithic applications into smaller, independent services that can be developed, deployed, and scaled individually
Event-Driven Architecture (EDA) — designing systems around events that trigger responses from multiple components or services
API First Design — designing APIs as the primary interface for interacting with the system, rather than traditional UIs
Cloud-Native Architecture — designing systems to take advantage of cloud computing benefits, such as scalability and on-demand resources
Containerization (Docker) — packaging applications and their dependencies into containers that can be easily deployed and managed
Service Mesh (Istio, Linkerd) — managing service communication and traffic in microservices architectures using a dedicated infrastructure layer
API Gateway Pattern — using an API gateway to manage API requests, authentication, and rate limiting

knowledge.what_it_is: Enterprise Architecture (EA) is a discipline that focuses on aligning business strategy with IT infrastructure and systems, ensuring scalability, security, and maintainability. It involves designing and implementing architectures for large-scale organizations, integrating multiple systems and technologies to achieve business goals.
what_you_will_learn: Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA) — designing applications as a collection of services that communicate with each other using APIs
Microservices Architecture — breaking down monolithic applications into smaller, independent services that can be scaled and deployed independently
Event-Driven Architecture (EDA) — designing systems around events and messages, enabling loose coupling and scalability
Domain-Driven Design (DDD) — focusing on business domain and processes to drive architecture decisions, using bounded contexts and value objects
Service Mesh — managing service communication and traffic with tools like Istio or Linkerd, enabling features like circuit breaking and request routing
API Gateway — acting as an entry point for external requests, handling authentication, rate limiting, and API key management
Cloud-Native Architecture — designing applications to take advantage of cloud computing models, using services like AWS Lambda or Azure Functions
Containerization with Docker — packaging applications and dependencies into containers for consistent deployment and scaling

knowledge.what_it_is: Design and architecture refers to the high-level structure and organization of software systems, encompassing both technical and business aspects. As a Software Architect, you will be responsible for designing scalable, maintainable, and efficient systems that meet the needs of stakeholders.
what_you_will_learn: Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA) — designing systems as a collection of services that communicate with each other using APIs
Microservices Architecture — breaking down monolithic applications into smaller, independent services that can be developed, deployed, and scaled individually
Event-Driven Architecture (EDA) — designing systems around events and message queues to enable loose coupling and scalability
Domain-Driven Design (DDD) — applying a modeling approach that focuses on the core business domain and its processes to drive software design decisions
API Gateway Pattern — using an API gateway as a single entry point for clients to access multiple services, providing security, rate limiting, and caching
CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation) — separating read and write operations into different models to improve scalability and performance
Event Sourcing — storing the history of application state changes as a sequence of events to enable auditing, debugging, and recovery
API Security — implementing authentication, authorization, rate limiting, and input validation for APIs to prevent attacks and data breaches

knowledge.what_it_is: Decision making is a critical skill for software architects, enabling them to make informed choices about system design, trade-offs between competing requirements, and optimize overall system performance. It involves analyzing complex data, considering multiple perspectives, and selecting the best course of action.
what_you_will_learn: Cost-benefit analysis — weighing the pros and cons of different design options, including development time, resource allocation, and long-term maintainability
Decision trees — visualizing and evaluating decision-making processes using tree-like structures, often in conjunction with data analytics tools like Tableau or Power BI
Root cause analysis — identifying and addressing underlying causes of problems, rather than just treating symptoms, using techniques like the 5 Whys or Fishbone diagrams
SWOT analysis — assessing strengths, weaknesses, opportunities, and threats to inform strategic decision-making, often in conjunction with business stakeholders
Value stream mapping — visualizing and optimizing the flow of value through a system, including identifying bottlenecks and areas for improvement
Design patterns for decision making — applying established design patterns like the Strategy pattern or Observer pattern to support informed decision-making
Metrics-driven development — using data and metrics to drive development decisions, such as A/B testing or experimentation
Risk management frameworks — applying structured approaches like ISO 31000 or NIST SP 800-30 to identify, assess, and mitigate risks

knowledge.what_it_is: Simplification is a key aspect of software architecture, aiming to reduce complexity and improve maintainability by breaking down complex systems into smaller, more manageable components. This involves identifying and eliminating unnecessary complexity, reducing coupling between components, and improving modularity.
what_you_will_learn: Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA) — designing applications as a collection of services that communicate with each other using APIs
Microservices Architecture — structuring an application as a collection of small, independent services that work together to provide the desired functionality
Event-Driven Architecture (EDA) — designing systems around events and their processing, enabling loose coupling between components
Domain-Driven Design (DDD) — focusing on the core business domain and modeling it using bounded contexts and value objects
CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation) — separating command handling from query handling to improve scalability and maintainability
Event Sourcing — storing application state as a sequence of events, enabling efficient auditing and debugging
API Gateways — acting as an entry point for clients to access multiple services, providing security, routing, and caching
Service Meshes — managing service communication using a dedicated infrastructure layer, providing features like traffic management and observability

knowledge.what_it_is: Marketing skills are essential for Software Architects to effectively communicate technical solutions to non-technical stakeholders, including product managers, sales teams, and customers. This involves understanding the business context, identifying market opportunities, and developing strategies to drive adoption of software products.
what_you_will_learn: Customer segmentation — defining target audiences based on demographics, behavior, and firmographics
Value proposition development — crafting clear, concise messaging that communicates unique value to customers
Competitor analysis — identifying market gaps and opportunities through SWOT analysis and competitor profiling
Marketing funnel optimization — streamlining the customer journey from awareness to conversion using data-driven insights
Content marketing strategy — developing a content calendar and creating engaging, relevant content for target audiences
Influencer outreach and partnerships — building relationships with industry thought leaders and leveraging their networks
Sales enablement — equipping sales teams with the knowledge, tools, and collateral needed to effectively sell software products
subtopics: digital marketing channels (paid social, email, search)

knowledge.what_it_is: Balance refers to achieving equilibrium between competing demands, priorities, and constraints in software development. As a Software Architect, balance involves making informed decisions that optimize system performance, scalability, reliability, security, and maintainability.
what_you_will_learn: Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA) — designing systems as loosely-coupled services to promote modularity and flexibility
Microservices Architecture — breaking down monolithic applications into smaller, independent services for greater agility and resilience
CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation) Pattern — separating read and write operations to improve system performance and scalability
Event Sourcing — storing application state as a sequence of events to enable auditing, debugging, and system evolution
API Gateway Pattern — centralizing API management to simplify security, rate limiting, and traffic control
Caching Strategies — implementing caching mechanisms (e.g., Redis, Memcached) to improve system performance and reduce latency
Load Balancing Algorithms — selecting optimal load balancing algorithms (e.g., Round-Robin, Least Connection) for efficient resource utilization
Service Discovery Mechanisms — using service discovery tools (e.g., etcd, Consul) to manage service instances and configurations

knowledge.what_it_is: As a Software Architect, consulting and coaching are essential skills to effectively communicate technical decisions to stakeholders, lead cross-functional teams, and drive architectural vision.
what_you_will_learn: Agile Coaching — applying Scrum or Kanban principles to facilitate team collaboration, iteration planning, and retrospectives
Technical Debt Management — identifying, prioritizing, and mitigating technical debt through code reviews, refactoring, and automation
Architecture Decision Records (ADRs) — documenting architectural decisions with justification, trade-offs, and alternatives for future reference
Solution Architecture Patterns — applying patterns like Microservices, Event-Driven Architecture, or Service-Oriented Architecture to complex systems
Coaching Frameworks — using frameworks like GROW (Goal, Reality, Options, Way forward) or SOAR (Strengths, Opportunities, Aspirations, Results) to facilitate coaching conversations
Stakeholder Management — identifying, analyzing, and influencing stakeholder needs, expectations, and impact on architectural decisions
Communication Strategies — developing effective communication plans, including presentation design, storytelling techniques, and conflict resolution
Team Leadership — leading cross-functional teams through change management, team building, and performance improvement initiatives

knowledge.what_it_is: Coding skills are essential for software architects to design, develop, and maintain large-scale software systems. As a software architect, you will be responsible for writing clean, efficient, and scalable code that meets the requirements of the project.
what_you_will_learn: Version control — managing changes to source code using Git, SVN, or Mercurial
Code organization — structuring code into modules, packages, and namespaces in languages like Java, C#, Python, or JavaScript
Design patterns — applying creational (Singleton, Factory), structural (Adapter, Bridge), and behavioral (Observer, Strategy) patterns to solve complex problems
Object-oriented programming (OOP) principles — encapsulation, inheritance, polymorphism, abstraction, and composition in languages like Java, C#, or Python
Type systems — understanding static typing (Java, C#), dynamic typing (Python, JavaScript), and type inference (Haskell, Rust)
Functional programming concepts — immutability, recursion, higher-order functions, and function composition in languages like Haskell, Lisp, or Scala
Testing frameworks — writing unit tests with JUnit, NUnit, PyUnit, or Jest, and integration tests with Cucumber, Selenium, or Cypress

knowledge.what_it_is: Documentation is the process of creating and maintaining accurate, up-to-date, and easily accessible documentation for software systems, ensuring that stakeholders understand system functionality, configuration, and operation.
what_you_will_learn: API Documentation — writing clear, concise descriptions of APIs using tools like Swagger, API Blueprint, or OpenAPI
System Design Documentation — creating detailed diagrams and descriptions of system architecture, including components, interfaces, and data flows
Code Comments — writing high-quality comments in code to explain complex logic, algorithms, and design decisions
Readme Files — crafting clear, concise README files for repositories, including setup instructions, usage examples, and troubleshooting tips
Documentation Tools — selecting and using tools like Confluence, Notion, or GitBook to manage documentation across teams and projects
Style Guides — creating and enforcing style guides for documentation, including formatting, tone, and consistency
Knowledge Graphs — building knowledge graphs to visualize relationships between components, services, and data entities
Technical Writing Best Practices — applying best practices for technical writing, including clarity, concision, and audience awareness

knowledge.what_it_is: Effective communication is crucial for software architects to collaborate with cross-functional teams, stakeholders, and end-users. It involves conveying complex technical ideas, requirements, and design decisions through various mediums such as written documents, presentations, and verbal discussions.
what_you_will_learn: Agile storytelling — using narratives to convey complex technical concepts and requirements
Technical writing — crafting clear, concise documentation for APIs, data models, and system architecture
Presentations and pitches — creating engaging slides, demos, and talks to communicate design decisions and technical trade-offs
Requirements gathering and elicitation techniques — using tools like MoSCoW prioritization, Kano model, and user story mapping
Communication protocols for distributed teams — using tools like Slack, Microsoft Teams, or Asana for real-time collaboration and feedback
Conflict resolution and negotiation strategies — managing disagreements and trade-offs with stakeholders and team members
API documentation and schema design — creating clear, machine-readable documentation for APIs using tools like Swagger or API Blueprint
Design decision documentation — recording and justifying technical choices and trade-offs in a shared knowledge base

knowledge.what_it_is: GitHub is a web-based platform for version control and collaboration on software development projects, allowing developers to manage code repositories, track changes, and collaborate with others.
what_you_will_learn: Git — understanding the fundamental concepts of Git, including commits, branches, merges, and pull requests
Repository management — creating, cloning, and managing GitHub repositories using the command line or GUI clients like GitHub Desktop
Pull Requests (PRs) — creating, reviewing, and merging PRs to manage changes and collaborate with team members
Forking and branching — understanding how to fork a repository, create branches, and merge changes back into the main branch
Collaboration tools — using features like GitHub Issues, Projects, and Wiki to facilitate collaboration and project management
Code reviews — participating in code reviews, providing feedback, and implementing suggestions from peers
GitHub Actions — automating workflows and continuous integration/continuous deployment (CI/CD) pipelines using GitHub's built-in automation tool
Secrets management — securely storing and managing sensitive data like API keys and credentials using GitHub's secrets feature

knowledge.what_it_is: Estimation and evaluation are crucial skills for software architects to ensure projects meet requirements, timelines, and budgets. It involves breaking down complex systems into manageable components, identifying dependencies, and creating realistic estimates based on historical data and industry benchmarks.
what_you_will_learn: Agile estimation techniques — using story points, planning poker, or relative sizing to estimate effort
Velocity calculation — determining team's average output per iteration to inform future estimates
Estimation metrics — tracking lead time, cycle time, and throughput to measure team performance
Burn-down charts — visualizing remaining work against planned schedule to identify potential issues
Earned Value Management (EVM) — comparing actual cost of work performed to planned value to evaluate project progress
Monte Carlo simulations — using statistical models to forecast project timelines and resource allocation
Dependency management — identifying and mitigating dependencies between components to reduce estimation uncertainty
Risk-based estimation — incorporating risk factors into estimates to account for potential issues

knowledge.what_it_is: Unified Modeling Language (UML) is an industry-standard language for software modeling, used to create visual representations of software systems, including class diagrams, sequence diagrams, and state machines. It provides a common notation for specifying, visualizing, constructing, and documenting the architecture of object-oriented systems.
what_you_will_learn: Class Diagrams — creating static structure diagrams to show relationships between classes, attributes, and methods
Object-Oriented Analysis (OOA) — applying UML principles to identify objects, attributes, and operations in a system
Use Case Diagrams — modeling interactions between actors and systems using use cases and extensions
Sequence Diagrams — illustrating the sequence of messages exchanged between objects over time
Activity Diagrams — showing the flow of control among activities and actions within a system
State Machine Diagrams — modeling the behavior of an object or system as it transitions through different states
Component Diagrams — visualizing the relationships between components, including interfaces and dependencies

knowledge.what_it_is: Technical skills are essential for software architects to design, develop, and maintain complex systems. They encompass a broad range of knowledge areas, including programming languages, frameworks, databases, cloud platforms, DevOps tools, and more.
what_you_will_learn: Design Patterns — applying creational (Singleton, Factory), structural (Adapter, Bridge), and behavioral (Observer, Strategy) patterns to improve system architecture
Microservices Architecture — designing loosely-coupled systems with multiple services, APIs, and databases for scalability and fault tolerance
Containerization and Orchestration — using Docker, Kubernetes, and Helm to containerize applications, manage dependencies, and automate deployment
Cloud Computing Platforms — architecting and deploying applications on AWS (EC2, S3, Lambda), Azure (VMs, Storage, Functions), Google Cloud (Compute Engine, Storage, Cloud Functions)
Database Systems — designing and implementing relational databases (MySQL, PostgreSQL), NoSQL databases (MongoDB, Cassandra), and graph databases (Neo4j) for efficient data storage and retrieval
API Design Principles — creating RESTful APIs with HTTP methods, URI conventions, request/response formats, and error handling for scalable communication between services
Security Best Practices — implementing authentication (OAuth2, JWT), authorization (RBAC, ABAC), encryption (TLS, SSL), and access control to protect sensitive data and systems
DevOps Tools — using Jenkins, GitLab CI/CD, CircleCI, or Travis CI for continuous integration, automated testing, and deployment of applications

knowledge.what_it_is: The .NET Framework is a software framework developed by Microsoft that provides a comprehensive set of libraries and tools for building Windows-based applications, including web, mobile, and desktop apps. It includes features like garbage collection, type safety, and memory management.
what_you_will_learn: ASP.NET MVC — a pattern-based approach to building web applications using controllers, views, and models
.NET Core — the cross-platform, open-source version of .NET that supports Windows, Linux, and macOS
Entity Framework Core — an ORM (Object-Relational Mapping) tool for interacting with databases using C# code
ADO.NET — a data access technology for connecting to various databases and executing queries
.NET Remoting — a framework for building distributed applications using remote objects and interfaces
WCF (Windows Communication Foundation) — a service-oriented architecture for building web services and APIs
Dependency Injection (DI) — a design pattern for decoupling components and promoting loose coupling

knowledge.what_it_is: Java, Kotlin, and Scala are three popular programming languages used by software architects to design and develop scalable, maintainable, and efficient systems. Java is an object-oriented language with a vast ecosystem of libraries and frameworks, while Kotlin is a modern language that runs on the JVM and provides null safety and coroutines. Scala is a multi-paradigm language that combines object-oriented and functional programming features.
what_you_will_learn: Java OOP concepts — encapsulation, inheritance, polymorphism, abstraction
Kotlin Coroutines — writing asynchronous code using suspend functions and coroutines builders
Scala Type System — understanding type inference, implicit conversions, and higher-kinded types
Java Design Patterns — applying creational (Factory Method, Singleton), structural (Adapter, Bridge), and behavioral (Observer, Strategy) patterns to software design
Kotlin Extensions — using extension functions to add behavior to existing classes without inheritance
Scala Macros — writing custom macros to generate boilerplate code at compile-time
Java Multithreading — creating and managing threads using ExecutorService, ThreadLocal, and synchronized blocks
Kotlin Data Classes — using data classes for simple data modeling and serialization

knowledge.what_it_is: Python is a high-level, interpreted programming language widely used by software architects for building scalable and maintainable systems. It's known for its simplicity, readability, and large standard library.
what_you_will_learn: Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) concepts — classes, objects, inheritance, polymorphism
Decorators — a design pattern for modifying function behavior without changing the original code
Generators and Iterators — efficient data processing using lazy loading and iteration protocols
Asyncio library — asynchronous I/O operations using coroutines and event loops
Type Hints and Static Type Checking — improving code maintainability with type annotations and tools like mypy
Context Managers — resource management using the `with` statement for exception safety and cleanup
Logging and Debugging — effective error handling and debugging techniques using logging frameworks like Loguru

knowledge.what_it_is: Ruby is a high-level, dynamic programming language used for building scalable and maintainable software systems, particularly in the context of web development and enterprise architecture. As a Software Architect, you'll use Ruby to design and implement robust, efficient, and secure systems that meet business requirements.
what_you_will_learn: MVC Pattern — Model-View-Controller architecture for separating concerns and promoting code reusability
Ruby on Rails Framework — using the popular framework for rapid web development with features like CRUD operations, routing, and authentication
Active Record Pattern — mapping database tables to Ruby objects for easier data access and manipulation
Dependency Injection — implementing loose coupling between components through external dependencies
Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA) — designing systems as a collection of services that communicate with each other using APIs
Domain-Driven Design (DDD) — applying principles like Bounded Contexts, Entities, and Value Objects to model complex business domains
Ruby's Metaprogramming Features — leveraging techniques like method_missing, define_method, and class_eval for dynamic code generation and modification
Testing with RSpec and Minitest — using popular testing frameworks for writing unit tests, integration tests, and behavioral tests

knowledge.what_it_is: Go, also known as Golang, is an open-source programming language developed by Google. It was designed to be simple, efficient, and easy to use, with a focus on concurrency and parallelism. Go is widely used for building scalable and reliable systems, including cloud infrastructure, network services, and distributed databases.
what_you_will_learn: Concurrency — using goroutines and channels for lightweight threading and communication
Go Modules — managing dependencies with the official module system, replacing GOPATH
Error Handling — using errors as values, not exceptions, with a focus on explicit error propagation
Type System — understanding Go's type safety features, including interfaces, structs, and generics
Concurrency Patterns — applying patterns like fan-in/fan-out, pipeline processing, and worker pools
Go Standard Library — mastering the built-in libraries for networking (net), file I/O (io), and concurrency (sync)
Testing in Go — writing unit tests with Go's built-in testing package and mocking frameworks like testify
Code Organization — structuring large codebases using packages, modules, and GOPATH

knowledge.what_it_is: JavaScript and TypeScript are high-level, dynamically-typed programming languages used for building scalable, maintainable, and efficient software systems. As a Software Architect, you will use JavaScript/TypeScript to design and implement complex architectures, APIs, and microservices.
what_you_will_learn: Type Inference — understanding how TypeScript infers types from code, enabling static type checking without explicit annotations
Modules and Imports — using ES6 modules, CommonJS, or UMD to organize and reuse code, with a focus on tree-shaking and dead-code elimination
Async/Await and Promises — mastering asynchronous programming with callbacks, promises, and async/await syntax for efficient concurrency management
Class-based vs. Functional Programming — understanding the trade-offs between object-oriented and functional programming paradigms in JavaScript/TypeScript
Decorators and Metaprogramming — using TypeScript's decorator syntax to implement AOP, logging, and other meta-programming techniques
Type Guards and Conditional Types — mastering advanced type manipulation with type guards, conditional types, and mapped types for robust type safety
Generics and Type Parameters — understanding how generics enable reusable, type-safe code through parameterized types and type inference
ES6+ Features and Syntax — familiarizing yourself with modern JavaScript features like let/const, arrow functions, destructuring, and template literals

knowledge.what_it_is: As a Software Architect, you will learn to utilize various tools and technologies to design, develop, and deploy software systems. This includes understanding the strengths and weaknesses of different programming languages, frameworks, and libraries.
what_you_will_learn: Version Control Systems — Git: managing code changes, collaborating with teams, and tracking project history
Agile Project Management Tools — Jira, Asana: planning, tracking, and reporting on software development projects
API Gateway and Service Mesh — AWS API Gateway, Istio: designing and implementing scalable microservices architectures
Containerization and Orchestration — Docker, Kubernetes: packaging, deploying, and managing containerized applications
Cloud Platforms and Services — AWS, Azure, Google Cloud: designing and deploying cloud-based software systems
Continuous Integration and Continuous Deployment (CI/CD) Tools — Jenkins, Travis CI: automating testing, building, and deployment processes
Monitoring and Logging Tools — Prometheus, Grafana, ELK Stack: collecting, analyzing, and visualizing system performance metrics
Security and Compliance Tools — OWASP ZAP, Burp Suite: identifying and mitigating security vulnerabilities in software systems

knowledge.what_it_is: Patterns and design principles are essential tools for software architects to create maintainable, scalable, and efficient systems. They provide a proven solution to common problems, allowing architects to focus on high-level system design rather than reinventing the wheel.
what_you_will_learn: SOLID principles — Single responsibility, Open/closed, Liskov substitution, Interface segregation, Dependency inversion: ensure modularity and flexibility in software systems
Creational patterns — Factory method, Abstract factory, Builder, Prototype: encapsulate object creation logic and improve extensibility
Structural patterns — Adapter, Bridge, Composite, Decorator, Facade: simplify complex relationships between objects and improve system maintainability
Behavioral patterns — Observer, Strategy, Template method, Visitor: decouple objects from specific actions and behaviors
Microservices architecture — Service discovery, Load balancing, Circuit breaking, API gateways: enable scalable and resilient distributed systems
Event-driven design — Event sourcing, CQRS, Sagas: manage complex business logic and improve system responsiveness
Domain-Driven Design (DDD) principles — Bounded contexts, Entities, Value objects, Aggregates: model complex domains and improve system maintainability

knowledge.what_it_is: Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) is a fundamental concept in software development that revolves around organizing code into objects and classes, promoting modularity, reusability, and maintainability. As a Software Architect, understanding OOP principles is crucial for designing scalable, efficient, and flexible software systems.
what_you_will_learn: Encapsulation — hiding internal implementation details of an object from the outside world using access modifiers (public, private, protected)
Abstraction — exposing only necessary information to the outside world while concealing implementation details
Inheritance — creating a new class based on an existing one, inheriting its properties and behavior
Polymorphism — allowing objects of different classes to be treated as if they were of the same class
Composition — combining multiple objects to form a more complex object
Single Responsibility Principle (SRP) — ensuring each class has only one reason to change
Open-Closed Principle (OCP) — designing classes that can be extended without modifying their source code
Dependency Injection (DI) — decoupling objects from dependencies using interfaces and dependency injection containers

knowledge.what_it_is: An Actor is a design pattern that allows for loose coupling between objects, enabling them to communicate with each other without being tightly coupled. In software architecture, Actors are used to model concurrent systems and provide a way to handle multiple requests simultaneously.
what_you_will_learn: Actor Model — the core concept of Actors, where each Actor is an independent entity that can receive and process messages concurrently
Mailbox Pattern — the mechanism by which Actors receive and manage incoming messages, allowing for efficient handling of concurrent requests
Actor Identity — a unique identifier assigned to each Actor, enabling them to be addressed and communicated with
Actor Location Transparency — the ability of Actors to communicate with each other without knowing their physical location or implementation details
Event-Driven Architecture (EDA) — a design pattern that uses Actors to handle events and notifications in a decoupled manner
CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation) Pattern — an extension of the Actor Model, used for handling commands and queries separately
Actor Systems — a collection of Actors working together to achieve a common goal, often using distributed systems and microservices architecture

knowledge.what_it_is: Reactive programming is an asynchronous data processing paradigm that allows software architects to design systems that respond efficiently and elegantly to changing inputs, outputs, or events. It's a key concept in modern software development, used extensively in cloud-native applications, real-time analytics, and IoT systems.
what_you_will_learn: Monadic programming — using monads (e.g., Maybe, Either) to manage computations that may fail or produce multiple results
Functional reactive programming (FRP) — combining functional programming with reactive streams for efficient event handling and data processing
ReactiveX (RxJava/RxSwift/RxCocoa) — a popular library for building reactive systems in Java/Kotlin, Swift, and Objective-C
Project Reactor — a modern reactive library for building reactive systems on the Spring platform
Akka Streams — a toolkit for building concurrent, distributed, and fault-tolerant systems using reactive streams
Reactive Extensions (Rx.NET) — a .NET library for building reactive systems using LINQ-style query operations
Event-driven architecture (EDA) — designing systems that respond to events in real-time, enabling loose coupling and scalability
CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation) — separating read and write concerns in reactive systems for improved performance and maintainability

knowledge.what_it_is: Functional programming (FP) is a paradigm that emphasizes immutability, purity, and compositionality to write more predictable, maintainable, and composable code. It's essential for software architects to understand FP principles to design scalable, modular, and fault-tolerant systems.
what_you_will_learn: Immutable data structures — using libraries like Immutable.js or Mori to ensure thread-safety and predictability
Pure functions — writing functions that always return the same output given the same inputs, without side effects
Higher-order functions (HOFs) — passing functions as arguments to other functions, enabling code reuse and modularity
Map, filter, reduce — using these fundamental array operations to process data in a declarative way
Recursion vs. iteration — understanding the trade-offs between recursive and iterative approaches for solving problems
Closures — using lexical scoping to capture variables and create encapsulated state
Currying — transforming functions with multiple arguments into nested functions with single arguments
Monads — applying a type of design pattern that abstracts away low-level details, enabling more composable code

knowledge.what_it_is: SOLID is an acronym describing five design principles of object-oriented programming (OOP) that aim to promote cleaner, more maintainable code and reduce coupling between classes. SOLID was first introduced by Michael Feathers as a mnemonic device to remember the key characteristics of good OOP design.
what_you_will_learn: Single Responsibility Principle (SRP) — each class should have only one reason to change, ensuring a single responsibility per class
Open-Closed Principle (OCP) — classes should be open for extension but closed for modification, allowing new functionality without changing existing code
Liskov Substitution Principle (LSP) — derived classes should be substitutable for their base classes, ensuring polymorphism and avoiding tight coupling
Interface Segregation Principle (ISP) — clients should not be forced to depend on interfaces they do not use, promoting loose coupling and flexibility
Dependency Inversion Principle (DIP) — high-level modules should not depend on low-level modules, but both should depend on abstractions
Applying SOLID principles in microservices architecture — how to apply SOLID principles when designing and implementing microservices
Using SOLID principles with containerization (e.g., Docker) — how to use SOLID principles when building containerized applications

knowledge.what_it_is: Test-Driven Development (TDD) is an iterative software development process that relies on the repetitive cycle of writing automated tests before writing the actual code. It ensures that each piece of code is thoroughly tested and validated, reducing bugs and improving overall system reliability.
what_you_will_learn: Unit testing — writing individual unit tests for small pieces of code to ensure they behave as expected
Integration testing — combining multiple units together to test how they interact with each other
Mocking dependencies — using libraries like Mockito or Moq to isolate dependencies and focus on the unit under test
Test doubles — creating fake implementations of dependencies to make tests more efficient and reliable
Behavior-Driven Development (BDD) — writing tests in a natural language style, focusing on the behavior of the system rather than its implementation details
Property-based testing — using libraries like QuickCheck or Hypothesis to generate test cases based on properties of the code
Test-driven API design — designing APIs with testing in mind, ensuring they are easy to test and maintain

knowledge.what_it_is: Domain-Driven Design (DDD) is an approach to software development that focuses on understanding and modeling complex business domains, emphasizing domain expertise and collaboration between developers and domain experts. It was introduced by Eric Evans in his 2003 book and has since become a widely adopted paradigm in software architecture.
what_you_will_learn: Domain Model — the core concept of DDD, representing the business domain as a set of interconnected objects and value objects
Entities — objects that have identity and can be uniquely identified, e.g., Customer, Order
Value Objects — immutable objects that represent a set of values, e.g., Money, Address
Aggregates — groups of related entities and value objects, defining the boundaries within which transactions are executed
Repositories — abstraction over data access, encapsulating data storage and retrieval logic
Domain Events — notifications about significant business events, used for decoupling and auditing
Bounded Contexts — independent sub-domains with their own models and languages, facilitating modular design
Ubiquitous Language — the shared language between developers and domain experts, ensuring everyone speaks the same 'domain dialect'
Factory Methods — encapsulating complex creation logic for entities and value objects

knowledge.what_it_is: MVC, MVP, and MVVM are architectural patterns used to separate application logic into interconnected components, improving maintainability, scalability, and reusability. MVC (Model-View-Controller) is a classic pattern used for web development, while MVP (Model-View-Presenter) and MVVM (Model-View-ViewModel) are more modern variants.
what_you_will_learn: MVC architecture — separating application logic into Model, View, and Controller components, with the Controller acting as an intermediary between the user interface and business logic
MVP pattern — using a Presenter to encapsulate business logic and separate it from the View, improving testability and maintainability
MVVM framework — utilizing a ViewModel to expose the data model in a form that is easily consumable by the View, while keeping the underlying data model unchanged
Separation of Concerns (SoC) — principles for organizing code into distinct components, each responsible for a specific aspect of the application
Dependency Injection (DI) — technique for decoupling components and making them more modular and testable
Repository pattern — abstracting data access and encapsulating business logic in a single layer, improving maintainability and scalability
Command Query Responsibility Segregation (CQRS) — separating read and write operations into distinct layers, improving performance and scalability

knowledge.what_it_is: Command Query Responsibility Segregation (CQRS) and Eventual Consistency are architectural patterns used to improve scalability, performance, and maintainability of software systems. CQRS separates read and write operations into different models, allowing for more efficient data retrieval and manipulation. Eventual Consistency ensures that data is consistent across all nodes in a distributed system, eventually converging to a consistent state.
what_you_will_learn: CQRS pattern — separating read and write operations into different models to improve scalability and performance
Command-side handling — processing commands that modify the application's state, using event sourcing or transactional storage
Query-side handling — retrieving data from a separate model optimized for reads, using caching, indexing, or materialized views
Event Store — storing events in a centralized repository, allowing for auditing, debugging, and business intelligence
Saga pattern — managing long-running transactions across multiple services, ensuring atomicity and consistency
Eventual Consistency model — allowing data to be written before it's read, using techniques like vector clocks or conflict-free replicated data types (CRDTs)
Distributed Transactional Storage — using technologies like Apache Kafka, Amazon SQS, or Google Cloud Pub/Sub for event-driven architecture
CQRS Frameworks and Libraries — exploring popular libraries like MediatR, Akka.NET, or CQRS.NET for .NET developers

knowledge.what_it_is: Microservices is an architectural style that structures an application as a collection of small, independent services, each with its own processes and data storage, communicating with each other using lightweight protocols. This approach enables scalability, flexibility, and fault tolerance.
what_you_will_learn: Service discovery — using tools like Netflix's Eureka or Apache ZooKeeper to manage service registration and lookup
API Gateway — designing a centralized entry point for client requests, often implemented with NGINX, AWS API Gateway, or Google Cloud Endpoints
Containerization — packaging microservices in containers like Docker, Kubernetes, or rkt to ensure consistency and portability
Service mesh — using tools like Istio, Linkerd, or Consul to manage service communication, traffic management, and security
Event-driven architecture — designing systems that respond to events rather than traditional requests, often implemented with message brokers like RabbitMQ or Apache Kafka
Circuit breaking — implementing fail-safes to prevent cascading failures when a service is unresponsive, using libraries like Hystrix or Resilience4J
Service registry — managing the registration and discovery of services in a distributed system, often implemented with etcd or Consul

knowledge.what_it_is: ACID (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability) and CAP Theorem are fundamental concepts in distributed systems design, ensuring data integrity and consistency across multiple nodes. ACID ensures database transactions are processed reliably, while CAP Theorem trade-offs consistency for availability and partition tolerance.
what_you_will_learn: Atomicity — ensuring all or nothing of a transaction is committed to the database
Consistency — maintaining data integrity by enforcing rules and constraints across nodes
Isolation — preventing concurrent transactions from interfering with each other's execution
Durability — guaranteeing that once a transaction is committed, it cannot be rolled back
CAP Theorem — understanding the trade-offs between consistency (C), availability (A), and partition tolerance (P) in distributed systems design
BASE (Basically Available, Soft-state, Eventually consistent) — an alternative to CAP Theorem for designing fault-tolerant systems
Eventual Consistency — allowing data to converge eventually, even if not immediately available or consistent across nodes
Conflict-free Replicated Data Types (CRDTs) — designing data structures that can be replicated without conflicts in distributed systems

knowledge.what_it_is: Serverless computing is an event-driven, pay-as-you-go model where code runs without provisioning or managing servers. It's a key aspect of modern cloud architecture, enabling scalability and cost-effectiveness.
what_you_will_learn: Function as a Service (FaaS) — understanding the core concept of serverless computing, with AWS Lambda, Azure Functions, Google Cloud Functions
Event-driven architecture — designing systems that respond to events, using event sources like APIs, message queues, and file storage
Lambda functions — writing code that runs on demand in response to events, with support for multiple languages and frameworks
API Gateway — creating RESTful APIs that integrate with serverless functions, handling requests and responses
Serverless security — understanding the unique security challenges of serverless computing, including IAM roles, access control, and encryption
Monitoring and logging — using tools like CloudWatch, Azure Monitor, and Stackdriver to monitor function performance and troubleshoot issues

knowledge.what_it_is: Client-Server architecture is a design pattern where presentation logic (client) and business logic (server) are decoupled, enabling scalability, flexibility, and easier maintenance. It's commonly used in web applications, mobile apps, and enterprise software systems.
what_you_will_learn: Service-oriented architecture — designing services as autonomous, stateless, and loosely-coupled components
RESTful APIs — building resource-based APIs using HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) and URI structures
Microservices architecture — breaking down monolithic applications into smaller, independent services communicating via APIs
API Gateway pattern — acting as a single entry point for clients to access multiple microservices or APIs
Load Balancing — distributing incoming traffic across multiple servers to improve responsiveness and availability
Caching mechanisms — using in-memory caching (e.g., Redis) or edge caching (e.g., Cloudflare) to reduce latency and improve performance
Authentication and Authorization protocols — implementing OAuth, OpenID Connect, or JWT for secure user authentication and access control
subtopics: serverless computing with aws lambda or google cloud functions

knowledge.what_it_is: A layered architecture is a software design pattern that separates an application into multiple, independent layers, each responsible for a specific functionality or concern.
what_you_will_learn: Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA) — designing services as self-contained units with well-defined interfaces
Microkernel Architecture — separating the core operating system from applications and services
Layered Service Pattern — organizing services into distinct layers for scalability, maintainability, and reusability
API Gateway Pattern — exposing a unified API interface to clients while hiding internal complexity
Service Composition — combining multiple services to create a new service with enhanced functionality
Event-Driven Architecture (EDA) — using events to communicate between layers and enable loose coupling
CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation) — separating read and write operations into distinct layers for improved performance

knowledge.what_it_is: A distributed system is a collection of independent computers that appear to be a single, cohesive system to users. It allows for horizontal scaling, fault tolerance, and high availability, making it essential for modern software architecture.
what_you_will_learn: CAP Theorem — the fundamental trade-off between consistency, availability, and partition tolerance in distributed systems
Paxos Algorithm — a consensus algorithm used to achieve agreement among nodes in a distributed system
Raft Consensus Algorithm — an improved version of Paxos that is more efficient and easier to understand
Distributed Transaction Management — techniques for ensuring atomicity and consistency across multiple nodes
Service Discovery — mechanisms for dynamically discovering and registering services in a distributed system (e.g. etcd, ZooKeeper)
Load Balancing — strategies for distributing workload across multiple nodes to improve responsiveness and availability
Caching — techniques for reducing latency by storing frequently accessed data in a centralized cache (e.g. Redis, Memcached)
Distributed Locking — mechanisms for synchronizing access to shared resources across multiple nodes

knowledge.what_it_is: Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA) is an architectural style that structures an application as a collection of services, each with a specific business capability and well-defined interface. It emphasizes loose coupling, modularity, and reusability to improve maintainability, scalability, and flexibility.
what_you_will_learn: Service discovery — using tools like Netflix's Eureka or Apache ZooKeeper for dynamic service registration and lookup
API Gateway — designing a centralized entry point for API requests, often implemented with NGINX, AWS API Gateway, or Google Cloud Endpoints
Microservices communication protocols — understanding RESTful APIs, gRPC, and message queues like RabbitMQ or Apache Kafka
Service composition — using techniques like service orchestration (e.g., Docker Swarm) or choreography to combine services
Event-driven architecture — designing systems that respond to events, often implemented with event stores like Event Store or Axon Server
API security — implementing authentication and authorization mechanisms, such as OAuth 2.0, JWT, or API keys
Service monitoring and logging — using tools like Prometheus, Grafana, ELK Stack (Elasticsearch, Logstash, Kibana), or Splunk to monitor service performance and logs

knowledge.what_it_is: Hashing algorithms are one-way functions that transform input data of any size into a fixed-size output, known as a digest or message digest. They are used to verify data integrity and authenticity in software systems.
what_you_will_learn: SHA-256 — a widely used cryptographic hash function with a 256-bit output, suitable for digital signatures and data integrity checks
MD5 — a commonly used hash function with a 128-bit output, but considered insecure due to collisions and preimage attacks
BLAKE2 — a high-performance hash function with a variable output size, designed for use in cryptographic protocols and digital signatures
HMAC — a keyed-hash message authentication code that combines a secret key with a hash function (e.g., SHA-256) to verify data authenticity
Hash functions vs. MACs — understanding the difference between hash functions (one-way) and message authentication codes (two-way)
Collision resistance — evaluating the likelihood of two different inputs producing the same output hash value
Preimage attacks — defending against attacks that aim to find an input that produces a given output hash value

knowledge.what_it_is: Public Key Infrastructure (PKI) is a set of roles, policies, and procedures involved in the creation, management, distribution, and revocation of public-key pairs, digital certificates, and other security credentials. It enables secure communication over untrusted networks by providing a way to verify the identity of entities and ensure the integrity of data.
what_you_will_learn: Certificate Authorities (CAs) — trusted third-party organizations responsible for issuing and managing digital certificates
Digital Certificates — electronic documents that bind a public key to an entity's identity, used for authentication and encryption
Public Key Cryptography Algorithms — RSA, Elliptic Curve Cryptography (ECC), and others used for key exchange and data encryption
Certificate Revocation Lists (CRLs) — lists of revoked certificates, used to prevent the use of compromised or invalid certificates
Online Certificate Status Protocol (OCSP) — a protocol used to check the status of a certificate in real-time
X.509 Certificates — a widely-used standard for digital certificates, including fields such as subject, issuer, and public key
Certificate Transparency (CT) Logs — publicly available logs that record all issued certificates, ensuring transparency and accountability

knowledge.what_it_is: Auth strategies refer to the approaches and techniques used by software architects to design, implement, and secure authentication systems in applications. This includes managing user identities, access control, and session management.
what_you_will_learn: OAuth 2.0 — an authorization framework for delegated access, commonly used with APIs (e.g., Google Sign-In, Facebook Login)
OpenID Connect (OIDC) — an identity layer on top of OAuth 2.0, providing user authentication and profile information
JSON Web Tokens (JWT) — a compact, URL-safe means of representing claims between two parties (e.g., client-server, server-resource)
Session management using cookies vs. token-based authentication (e.g., JWT, session IDs)
Single Sign-On (SSO) — allowing users to access multiple applications with a single set of credentials
Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) — requiring users to provide additional verification factors beyond just a password
Password hashing and salting using algorithms like bcrypt, Argon2, or PBKDF2
API key management and validation for secure API access

knowledge.what_it_is: OWASP (Open Web Application Security Project) is an open-source, non-profit organization that provides free and publicly available documentation on web application security risks and countermeasures. As a Software Architect, understanding OWASP guidelines is crucial for designing secure web applications.
what_you_will_learn: OWASP Top 10 — the standard list of most critical web application security risks, updated annually
Injection Attacks — preventing SQL, NoSQL, and command injection vulnerabilities through parameterized queries and prepared statements
Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) Prevention — using Content Security Policy (CSP), HTML escaping, and input validation to prevent XSS attacks
Authentication and Authorization Best Practices — implementing secure authentication protocols like OAuth 2.0, OpenID Connect, and SAML
Secure Password Storage — using bcrypt, Argon2, or PBKDF2 for password hashing and salting
Secure Data Transmission — encrypting data in transit with TLS/SSL, including certificate management and pinning
Web Application Firewall (WAF) Configuration — configuring WAFs to protect against common web attacks like SQL injection and cross-site scripting

knowledge.what_it_is: ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) and data warehousing are critical components of a software architect's role, enabling data integration, processing, and analysis across various systems. ETL tools like Talend, Informatica PowerCenter, and Microsoft SQL Server Integration Services (SSIS) simplify the process by automating data extraction, transformation, and loading into a centralized repository.
what_you_will_learn: ETL pipeline design — designing efficient workflows for data processing, including data quality checks and error handling
Data modeling techniques — applying entity-relationship diagrams (ERDs), dimensional modeling, and star/snowflake schema designs to optimize data warehousing

Data warehousing architectures — understanding the differences between traditional relational databases, column-store databases like Amazon Redshift, and cloud-based data warehouses like Google BigQuery
ETL tool selection criteria — evaluating factors such as scalability, performance, data types supported, and integration with existing systems when choosing an ETL tool
Data transformation techniques — applying mapping, aggregation, and filtering to transform raw data into a usable format for analysis
Data quality metrics — defining and tracking key performance indicators (KPIs) for data accuracy, completeness, and consistency
Data governance frameworks — implementing policies and procedures for data security, access control, and compliance with regulations like GDPR and HIPAA

knowledge.what_it_is: SQL databases are relational databases that store and manage data using Structured Query Language (SQL). They are widely used in enterprise environments due to their scalability, reliability, and support for complex transactions. As a software architect, understanding SQL databases is crucial for designing robust and scalable systems.
what_you_will_learn: Database normalization — the process of organizing data into tables with minimal redundancy and dependency
Entity-relationship modeling (ERM) — creating diagrams to visualize relationships between entities in a database
SQL schema design — defining the structure of a database, including table relationships and constraints
Indexing and caching — optimizing query performance by indexing columns and using caching mechanisms
ACID compliance — ensuring database transactions are atomic, consistent, isolated, and durable
Database sharding — distributing data across multiple servers to improve scalability and performance
SQL injection prevention — techniques for protecting against malicious SQL code injection attacks
Query optimization — analyzing and optimizing SQL queries to reduce execution time and resource usage

knowledge.what_it_is: NoSQL databases are non-relational, distributed data stores that provide flexible schema designs and high scalability, making them suitable for big data and real-time web applications. They are commonly used in modern software architectures to handle large amounts of unstructured or semi-structured data.
what_you_will_learn: Key-value storage — storing data as a collection of key-value pairs, with keys being unique identifiers (e.g., Redis, Riak)
Document-oriented databases — storing data in self-describing documents, often using JSON-like formats (e.g., MongoDB, Couchbase)
Column-family stores — organizing data by columns instead of rows, optimized for high-performance analytics and reporting (e.g., Cassandra, HBase)
Graph databases — storing data as nodes and edges, suitable for complex relationships and queries (e.g., Neo4j, Amazon Neptune)
Cloud-native NoSQL databases — designed for cloud deployment, scalability, and multi-region consistency (e.g., Google Cloud Bigtable, Amazon DynamoDB)
Sharding and partitioning strategies — distributing data across multiple nodes or servers to improve performance and availability
Data modeling and schema design — designing efficient data structures and schemas for NoSQL databases

knowledge.what_it_is: APIs and integrations are crucial components of modern software architecture, enabling communication between different systems, services, and applications. As a Software Architect, you will design, implement, and maintain APIs to facilitate data exchange, business logic integration, and system interoperability.
what_you_will_learn: RESTful API design — designing APIs that follow the REST architectural style, using HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) for resource manipulation
API Gateway patterns — implementing an entry point for API requests, handling authentication, rate limiting, and routing to multiple microservices or backend systems
GraphQL vs. REST — understanding the trade-offs between GraphQL's schema-driven approach and traditional RESTful APIs, including query complexity, caching, and data fetching strategies
gRPC fundamentals — learning about gRPC's high-performance RPC framework, including protocol buffers, streaming, and service discovery
API Security best practices — implementing authentication (OAuth 2.0, JWT), authorization (RBAC, ABAC), and encryption (TLS, SSL) to protect APIs from unauthorized access and data breaches
Microservices architecture — designing systems composed of multiple services communicating over APIs, including service discovery, load balancing, and circuit breakers

Event-driven Architecture (EDA) — building systems that respond to events generated by other services or external sources, using message queues (RabbitMQ, Apache Kafka), event stores, and pub-sub patterns
API Documentation standards — generating and maintaining API documentation using tools like Swagger, OpenAPI, and API Blueprint, including code-first approaches and automated generation

knowledge.what_it_is: gRPC is a high-performance RPC framework developed by Google, designed to enable efficient and scalable communication between microservices. It uses Protocol Buffers (protobuf) for data serialization and provides features like streaming, load balancing, and service discovery.
what_you_will_learn: Protocol Buffers (protobuf) — defining data structures using .proto files and generating code in various languages
gRPC Services — designing RESTful APIs with RPC capabilities, including methods, request/response types, and error handling
gRPC Clients/Servers — building clients and servers using gRPC stubs, including asynchronous programming models and connection management
Streaming — implementing bidirectional streaming for real-time data exchange between services, including server-push and client-pull
Load Balancing — configuring load balancers to distribute traffic across multiple instances of a service, including round-robin, least connections, and IP hashing
Service Discovery — using service discovery mechanisms like etcd or Consul to manage service registration and lookup in a distributed system
Authentication/Authorization — integrating gRPC with authentication and authorization systems like OAuth2, JWT, or OpenID Connect

knowledge.what_it_is: GraphQL is a query language for APIs, designed to provide a more flexible and efficient way of data retrieval compared to traditional RESTful APIs. It was created by Facebook in 2015 and has since become widely adopted across various industries.
what_you_will_learn: Schema Definition Language (SDL) — defining the schema using GraphQL Schema Definition Language, including types, fields, and resolvers
Resolvers — functions that fetch data from a database or other backend services to fulfill GraphQL queries
Query Types — defining query types for retrieving data, such as Query, Mutation, and Subscription
Schema Stitching — combining multiple schemas into a single schema using the @extends directive
Type Extensions — extending existing types with new fields using the @extend directive
Interfaces — defining interfaces that specify a set of methods or properties that must be implemented by any type that implements it
Unions and Interfaces — using unions to represent a value that can be one of multiple types, and interfaces to define a contract for a type
Input Types — defining input types for mutations, including validation rules and default values

knowledge.what_it_is: Representational State of Resource (REST) is an architectural style for designing networked applications, emphasizing simplicity and scalability. It's widely adopted due to its flexibility and ease of implementation.
what_you_will_learn: Resource identification — using URIs to identify resources and their relationships
Client-server architecture — separating concerns between client and server, promoting loose coupling
Uniform interface — standardizing communication between components using HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE)
Statelessness — avoiding session management on the server-side for improved scalability
Cacheability — leveraging caching mechanisms to reduce latency and improve performance
Layered system — breaking down complex systems into smaller, independent layers for easier maintenance
Code on demand (optional) — dynamically loading code or configuration based on client requests
Uniform content encoding (optional) — standardizing data representation using formats like JSON, XML

knowledge.what_it_is: Enterprise Service Bus (ESB) and Simple Object Access Protocol (SOAP) are architectural patterns used to integrate systems and enable communication between them. ESB acts as a centralized hub for message routing, transformation, and security, while SOAP is a protocol for exchanging structured information in the implementation of web services.
what_you_will_learn: ESB architecture — designing a centralized hub for message routing, transformation, and security
Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA) principles — applying SOA to integrate systems and enable communication between them
SOAP protocol fundamentals — understanding SOAP messaging, envelopes, headers, bodies, and attachments
WSDL (Web Services Description Language) — defining service interfaces using WSDL for SOAP-based web services
UDDI (Universal Description, Discovery, and Integration) — registering and discovering web services in UDDI registries
Message transformation — using tools like XSLT or XMLSpy to transform messages between different formats
Security considerations — implementing authentication, authorization, and encryption for SOAP-based web services

knowledge.what_it_is: A data warehouse is a centralized repository that stores integrated data from multiple sources, providing business intelligence and analytics capabilities to support informed decision-making.
what_you_will_learn: Star schema — a logical data model for efficient querying and analysis of large datasets
Fact table — a central table containing measures (facts) with foreign keys referencing dimension tables
Dimension table — a table containing descriptive attributes (dimensions) used to slice and dice fact tables
Slowly changing dimensions (SCDs) — techniques for handling changes in dimension data over time, such as type 1, type 2, and hybrid SCDs
ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) processes — tools like Informatica, Talend, or AWS Glue for extracting, transforming, and loading data into the warehouse
Data mart — a subset of a data warehouse designed to meet specific business needs or support a particular application
OLAP (Online Analytical Processing) cubes — pre-aggregated data structures used for fast querying and analysis
Data warehousing vs. data marts: understanding when to use each approach

knowledge.what_it_is: A messaging queue is a data structure that allows messages to be sent and received asynchronously, decoupling producers from consumers and enabling loose coupling between systems. It's commonly used in distributed systems, microservices architecture, and event-driven design.
what_you_will_learn: Message brokers — understanding the role of message brokers like RabbitMQ, Apache Kafka, Amazon SQS, and Google Cloud Pub/Sub in managing messaging queues
Queue data structures — learning about different queue data structures such as First-In-First-Out (FIFO), Last-In-First-Out (LIFO), and priority queues
Message serialization — understanding how to serialize messages using formats like JSON, XML, or MessagePack for efficient transmission
Producer-consumer model — designing systems that use the producer-consumer pattern to handle message production and consumption
Message routing — learning about message routing mechanisms such as topic-based routing, header-based routing, and content-based routing
Dead-letter queues — understanding how to handle failed messages using dead-letter queues and implementing retry policies
Queue monitoring and metrics — learning about tools like Prometheus, Grafana, and New Relic for monitoring messaging queue performance and latency

knowledge.what_it_is: Hadoop, Spark, and MapReduce are distributed computing frameworks used for processing large datasets. Hadoop is a Java-based framework that stores data in a distributed file system (HDFS), while Spark is an in-memory computing engine that provides high-performance processing of big data. MapReduce is the programming model used by Hadoop to process data in parallel across a cluster.
what_you_will_learn: Distributed computing concepts — designing systems for scalability and fault tolerance
HDFS architecture — understanding how Hadoop stores and retrieves data from its distributed file system
MapReduce programming model — writing jobs that process data in parallel using mappers and reducers
Spark Core API — using Spark's high-level abstraction to write efficient, in-memory processing code
Data serialization formats — working with Avro, Parquet, and JSON for data exchange between Hadoop components
YARN architecture — understanding how Hadoop manages resources and schedules jobs across a cluster
Spark SQL and DataFrames — using Spark's high-level abstractions to work with structured data
Hadoop ecosystem tools — integrating with Pig, Hive, Flume, and Oozie for data processing and workflow management

knowledge.what_it_is: Learn management involves understanding how to plan, track, and optimize software development projects, ensuring they are delivered on time, within budget, and meet required quality standards. This includes using various tools, techniques, and methodologies to manage scope, schedule, resources, and risk.
what_you_will_learn: Agile project management — understanding Scrum and Kanban frameworks for iterative development
Waterfall methodology — planning and executing projects in a linear sequence with clear milestones and deliverables
Project Initiation Document (PID) — creating a comprehensive document outlining project scope, goals, and requirements
Work Breakdown Structure (WBS) — decomposing complex projects into manageable tasks and activities
Gantt charts — visualizing project schedules and dependencies using timelines and critical path analysis
Resource allocation and leveling — optimizing team assignments and workload to meet project demands
Risk management — identifying, assessing, and mitigating potential threats to project success
Earned Value Management (EVM) — tracking project performance against budget and schedule using metrics like CPI and SPI

knowledge.what_it_is: Networks are a crucial aspect of software architecture, enabling communication between systems and devices over the internet or within an organization's infrastructure. As a Software Architect, you will design and implement network architectures to meet the scalability, security, and performance requirements of your applications.
what_you_will_learn: TCP/IP Model — understanding the four-layer model (Application, Transport, Internet, Network) for data communication
OSI Reference Model — familiarizing yourself with the seven-layer model (Physical, Data Link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, Application) for network protocol interactions
Network Topologies — designing and implementing LANs (Local Area Networks), WANs (Wide Area Networks), MANs (Metropolitan Area Networks), and SANs (Storage Area Networks)
Subnetting and CIDR Notation — understanding how to divide IP addresses into subnets using Classless Inter-Domain Routing (CIDR) notation
Routing Protocols — learning about static routing, dynamic routing (RIP, OSPF, EIGRP), and routing protocols for IPv6 (RIPng, OSPFv3)
Switching and Routing Fundamentals — understanding the differences between Layer 2 (switching) and Layer 3 (routing) devices
Quality of Service (QoS) — designing QoS policies to prioritize network traffic based on parameters such as packet size, priority, and delay
Network Security — implementing firewalls, intrusion detection/prevention systems, and secure protocols for data transfer (HTTPS, SFTP)
Cloud Networking — understanding how to design and deploy virtual networks in cloud environments (AWS VPC, Azure Virtual Network, Google Cloud VPC)

knowledge.what_it_is: Linux and Unix are open-source operating systems that provide a robust, secure, and customizable foundation for software architecture. They are built on top of the POSIX standard, ensuring portability across different hardware platforms.
what_you_will_learn: Filesystem Hierarchy Standard (FHS) — understanding the directory structure and organization of Linux/Unix file systems
Process Management — managing processes using tools like ps, kill, and nice, as well as process scheduling algorithms
System Calls — understanding the system calls provided by the kernel, including open(), close(), read(), write(), and exec()
Permissions and Access Control Lists (ACLs) — managing file permissions and ACLs to ensure secure access control
Networking Fundamentals — configuring network interfaces, routing tables, and socket programming using tools like netstat, route, and ip
System Logging and Monitoring — setting up log files, monitoring system performance with tools like top, htop, and sar
Security Features — implementing security features like SELinux, AppArmor, and Linux capabilities to restrict user privileges
Scripting with Bash — writing shell scripts using bash syntax and built-in commands like if-else statements, loops, and functions

knowledge.what_it_is: The OSI (Open Systems Interconnection) model is a 7-layered framework used to standardize network communication, developed by ISO (International Organization for Standardization). It provides a hierarchical structure for understanding and designing network protocols, ensuring interoperability between different systems. As a Software Architect, you will work with the OSI layers to design scalable, efficient, and secure network architectures.
what_you_will_learn: Layer 1: Physical — Understanding physical layer components, such as cables, hubs, switches, and routers, and their roles in data transmission
Layer 2: Data Link — Familiarity with data link layer protocols (e.g., Ethernet, Wi-Fi), frame formats, and error detection/correction mechanisms
Layer 3: Network — Knowledge of network layer protocols (e.g., IP, ICMP, IGMP), routing algorithms, and address management (CIDR, subnetting)
Layer 4: Transport — Understanding transport layer protocols (e.g., TCP, UDP), connection establishment, and data transfer mechanisms
Layer 5: Session — Familiarity with session layer protocols (e.g., NetBIOS, SSH) and their role in establishing, maintaining, and terminating connections
