Layer 6: Presentation — Knowledge of presentation layer protocols (e.g., SSL/TLS, MIME) and data encoding/decoding techniques
Layer 7: Application — Understanding application layer protocols (e.g., HTTP, FTP, SMTP), client-server interactions, and service discovery mechanisms

knowledge.what_it_is: The TCP/IP model is a conceptual framework used by software architects to understand and design network communication protocols, consisting of four layers: Application, Transport, Internet, and Link. It provides a structured approach to building scalable and reliable network architectures.
what_you_will_learn: Layered architecture — understanding the modular structure of the TCP/IP model and its implications for system design
Application Layer (Layer 7) protocols — HTTP, FTP, SMTP, DNS, and their role in interacting with the Transport Layer
Transport Layer (Layer 4) protocols — TCP, UDP, SCTP, and their key features such as connection establishment, segmentation, and flow control
Internet Layer (Layer 3) protocols — IP, ICMP, IGMP, and their functions in routing packets between networks
Link Layer (Layer 2) protocols — Ethernet, Wi-Fi, PPP, and their responsibilities for framing, addressing, and error detection
Socket programming — creating and managing endpoints for network communication using APIs like Berkeley sockets or Winsock
TCP/IP header formats — understanding the structure and fields of TCP and IP headers, including source and destination addresses, protocol numbers, and checksums

knowledge.what_it_is: HTTP (Hypertext Transfer Protocol) and HTTPS (Hypertext Transfer Protocol Secure) are communication protocols used to transfer data over the internet. HTTP is an application-layer protocol that uses TCP/IP as its underlying transport layer, while HTTPS is a secure version of HTTP that uses SSL/TLS encryption to protect data integrity and confidentiality.
what_you_will_learn: HTTP Request/Response Cycle — understanding how clients send requests and servers respond with status codes (200-299), headers, and bodies
HTTP Methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, PATCH) — learning the difference between each method's purpose and use cases in software architecture
HTTP Status Codes (1xx, 2xx, 3xx, 4xx, 5xx) — understanding the meaning of each status code and how to handle them in error handling mechanisms
HTTPS Certificate Management — learning how to obtain, install, and manage SSL/TLS certificates for secure communication between clients and servers
HTTP Caching Mechanisms (Cache-Control, ETag, Last-Modified) — understanding how to implement caching to improve performance and reduce latency
HTTP/2 Multiplexing — learning about the benefits of multiplexing multiple requests over a single connection in HTTP/2
HTTP/3 QUIC Protocol — understanding the new transport protocol that combines the benefits of TCP, UDP, and HTTP/2 for faster and more secure communication
subtopics: http methods (get, post, put, delete, patch)
http request body encoding (url-encoded, json, etc.)
validation_keywords: http methods (get, post, put, delete, patch)
http request body encoding (url-encoded, json, etc.)

knowledge.what_it_is: Infrastructure as Code (IaC) is a practice of managing and provisioning infrastructure resources, such as virtual machines, networks, and storage, using code and configuration files. This approach enables software architects to define and version their infrastructure configurations, making it easier to manage changes and ensure consistency across different environments.
what_you_will_learn: IaC tools — Terraform, AWS CloudFormation, Azure Resource Manager, Google Cloud Deployment Manager: understanding the strengths and weaknesses of each tool
Infrastructure as Code (IaC) principles — defining infrastructure resources using code, versioning configurations, and managing changes through CI/CD pipelines
Terraform modules — reusing and composing infrastructure configurations to reduce duplication and improve maintainability
CloudFormation stacks — organizing and deploying cloud-based infrastructure resources in a single unit of work
Infrastructure configuration management — using tools like Ansible, SaltStack, or Puppet to manage and provision infrastructure resources
CI/CD pipeline integration — automating the deployment of IaC configurations through continuous integration and delivery pipelines

knowledge.what_it_is: Cloud providers are third-party companies that offer scalable, on-demand computing resources over the internet, enabling businesses to reduce capital expenditures and increase agility. Major cloud providers include Amazon Web Services (AWS), Microsoft Azure, Google Cloud Platform (GCP), IBM Cloud, and Oracle Cloud.
what_you_will_learn: Cloud Service Models — understanding IaaS, PaaS, SaaS, and their differences in terms of resource allocation and management
Cloud Deployment Models — learning about public, private, community, and hybrid clouds, including their use cases and trade-offs
AWS Well-Architected Framework — applying best practices for designing and operating reliable, secure, efficient, and cost-effective systems on AWS
Azure Resource Manager (ARM) templates — using JSON-based templates to define and deploy Azure resources in a repeatable and consistent manner
GCP Cloud Functions — building event-driven serverless applications using Google's cloud functions platform
Cloud Security Considerations — understanding key security risks and controls for cloud environments, including data encryption, access management, and compliance
Cloud Cost Optimization Strategies — learning techniques to minimize waste and maximize ROI in cloud deployments, such as right-sizing instances and leveraging spot pricing

knowledge.what_it_is: Serverless computing is an architectural pattern where applications are built without provisioning or managing servers, leveraging cloud providers' scalable infrastructure to handle workload spikes and reduce operational overhead.
what_you_will_learn: Event-driven architecture — designing systems around events instead of traditional request-response patterns
Function as a Service (FaaS) — building serverless applications using functions triggered by events or APIs
Serverless frameworks — using tools like AWS Lambda, Azure Functions, Google Cloud Functions to simplify serverless development
API Gateway — managing API traffic and security for serverless applications
Distributed tracing — monitoring and debugging distributed systems with tools like OpenTracing, Jaeger
Service integration — combining multiple services into a single, cohesive application using APIs and event-driven architecture
Security in serverless environments — understanding the unique security challenges of serverless computing and mitigating risks

knowledge.what_it_is: A service mesh is a dedicated infrastructure layer for microservices communication, providing features like service discovery, load balancing, traffic management, and observability. It's designed to simplify the complexity of distributed systems and improve their reliability, scalability, and security.
what_you_will_learn: Service Mesh Architecture — understanding the components and layers involved in a service mesh (e.g., data plane, control plane, sidecar proxies)
Istio vs. Linkerd vs. Consul Service Mesh — comparing popular service mesh implementations and their use cases
Sidecar Pattern — using a separate process to manage communication between services, decoupling them from the application code
Service Discovery — understanding how service meshes handle service registration, deregistration, and discovery (e.g., etcd, ZooKeeper)
Traffic Management — learning about routing rules, circuit breakers, and fault tolerance in service meshes (e.g., Istio's DestinationRule)
Observability — using service meshes to collect metrics, logs, and traces for distributed systems (e.g., Prometheus, Grafana)
Security Features — understanding how service meshes provide encryption, authentication, and authorization for microservices
Service Mesh Configuration — learning about configuration management tools like Kubernetes ConfigMaps and Secret resources

knowledge.what_it_is: Continuous Integration and Continuous Deployment (CI/CD) is a software development practice that automates testing, building, and deployment of code changes to production environments. As a Software Architect, you will be responsible for designing and implementing CI/CD pipelines to ensure smooth delivery of software updates.
what_you_will_learn: CI/CD pipeline architecture — designing multi-stage pipelines with automated testing, building, and deployment
Jenkinsfile syntax — writing declarative scripts in Jenkins to define build and deployment processes
Docker containerization — packaging applications and dependencies into containers for consistent deployment
Kubernetes orchestration — managing containerized applications across clusters using Kubernetes APIs
GitOps principles — using Git as the single source of truth for infrastructure configuration and application state
CI/CD toolchain selection — choosing between Jenkins, GitLab CI/CD, CircleCI, or GitHub Actions for pipeline automation
Automated testing strategies — implementing unit tests, integration tests, and end-to-end tests to ensure quality
Infrastructure as Code (IaC) with Terraform — defining cloud infrastructure using human-readable configuration files

knowledge.what_it_is: Containers are a lightweight and portable way to package, ship, and run applications, providing isolation, security, and resource efficiency. They use operating system-level virtualization to create a separate environment for each application, with its own file system, network stack, and process space.
what_you_will_learn: Docker containers — the industry standard for containerization, using Dockerfiles for build-time configuration
Kubernetes clusters — orchestrating and managing containerized applications at scale, using deployments, replicasets, and daemonsets
Container networking — connecting containers to a shared network stack, using CNI plugins like Calico or Flannel
Volume mounting — persisting data between container restarts, using bind mounts, tmpfs, or Docker volumes
Resource management — allocating CPU, memory, and other resources to containers, using cgroups and namespaces
Security best practices — securing containers with Docker security features like AppArmor, SELinux, and seccomp
Containerized logging — collecting logs from containers, using tools like Fluentd or Splunk
Service discovery — enabling communication between containers, using service registries like etcd or Consul

knowledge.what_it_is: Cloud design patterns are reusable solutions to common problems encountered when designing and building cloud-based systems, emphasizing scalability, reliability, and maintainability.
what_you_will_learn: Microservices Architecture — structuring applications as a collection of small, independent services communicating with each other using APIs
Event-Driven Architecture (EDA) — designing systems around events that trigger business processes, promoting loose coupling and scalability
Serverless Computing — building applications without provisioning or managing servers, leveraging cloud providers' resources for compute, storage, and databases
Containerization — packaging applications and their dependencies into containers for consistent deployment across environments
Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA) — structuring systems around services that expose APIs to other services, promoting reuse and modularity
API Gateway Pattern — designing a single entry point for clients to access multiple backend services, providing security, rate limiting, and analytics
Circuit Breaker Pattern — detecting and preventing cascading failures in distributed systems by implementing timeouts and fallbacks
Load Balancer Pattern — distributing incoming traffic across multiple servers or instances to improve responsiveness and availability

knowledge.what_it_is: A proxy is an intermediary that sits between a client and a server, forwarding requests and responses while modifying or adding functionality as needed. In software architecture, proxies are used to decouple clients from servers, improve security, and enhance performance.
what_you_will_learn: Transparent proxy — forwarding requests without modification, often used for load balancing
Reverse proxy — sitting between a client and multiple servers, routing requests based on server availability or configuration
Squid proxy — caching frequently accessed resources to reduce latency and improve performance
NGINX reverse proxy — using the NGINX web server as a reverse proxy to distribute traffic across multiple backend servers
Kubernetes ingress controller — configuring an ingress resource to define rules for routing incoming HTTP requests
Service mesh architecture — using proxies to manage service-to-service communication, including traffic management and security
gRPC proxy — using the gRPC framework to create a proxy that handles RPC calls between services
API gateway pattern — using a proxy to aggregate multiple APIs into a single entry point for clients

knowledge.what_it_is: A firewall is a network security system that monitors and controls incoming and outgoing traffic based on predetermined security rules, preventing unauthorized access to or from a private network. As a software architect, you will design and implement firewalls to protect your organization's infrastructure.
what_you_will_learn: Firewall types — understanding the differences between hardware-based (e.g., Cisco ASA), software-based (e.g., pfSense), and cloud-based (e.g., AWS Network Firewall) firewalls
Network Address Translation (NAT) — configuring NAT to allow multiple devices to share a single public IP address while hiding their private IP addresses from the internet
Stateful Packet Inspection (SPI) — implementing SPI to track the state of network connections and block unauthorized traffic based on that state
Access Control Lists (ACLs) — creating ACLs to define permit or deny rules for specific traffic flows, including source and destination IP addresses, ports, and protocols
Firewall rule prioritization — understanding how firewalls prioritize rules when multiple rules match a given traffic flow and how to optimize rule sets for performance
Logging and monitoring — setting up logging and monitoring tools (e.g., Splunk, ELK Stack) to analyze firewall activity and detect potential security threats
Firewall configuration management — using tools like Ansible or Terraform to automate and manage firewall configurations across multiple devices and environments

knowledge.what_it_is: Microsoft Dynamics (MSD) is an enterprise resource planning (ERP) and customer relationship management (CRM) software suite developed by Microsoft, designed to help organizations manage their business operations, customers, and finances. As a Software Architect, you will be responsible for designing and implementing MSD solutions that meet the organization's specific needs.
what_you_will_learn: MSD Architecture — understanding the layered architecture of MSD, including the application tier, database tier, and presentation tier
Entity Framework Core (EF Core) — using EF Core to interact with MSD databases, including entity modeling, data access, and change tracking
Business Process Flows (BPFs) — designing and implementing BPFs in MSD to automate business processes and workflows
Dynamics 365 Business Central (BC) — understanding the features and functionality of BC, including financial management, supply chain management, and project management
Power Apps (PA) — using PA to build custom applications on top of MSD data, including forms, views, and reports
Microsoft Power Automate (formerly Microsoft Flow) — automating business processes and workflows using Power Automate flows that integrate with MSD
MSD Security and Access Control — designing and implementing security and access control mechanisms in MSD, including role-based security, user authentication, and data encryption

knowledge.what_it_is: SAP ERP (Enterprise Resource Planning) is a comprehensive business software suite that integrates various functions of an organization, including financials, logistics, and human resources. SAP HANA is an in-memory relational database management system that provides real-time data processing capabilities. Business Objects is a business intelligence platform for reporting, analytics, and data visualization.
what_you_will_learn: SAP ERP architecture — understanding the layered structure of SAP ERP, including the application server, database, and web interface
HANA database concepts — learning about column-store storage, in-memory processing, and ACID compliance for real-time analytics
Business Objects BI platform — familiarizing with Web Intelligence, Crystal Reports, and Xcelsius for reporting and data visualization
SAP NetWeaver integration — understanding the role of SAP NetWeaver in integrating various SAP systems, including ERP, CRM, and SCM
HANA SQL syntax — learning about HANA's proprietary SQL dialect, including support for JSON and XML data types
Business Objects universe management — creating and managing universes to define business logic and data relationships
SAP ERP custom development — understanding the process of developing custom SAP ERP applications using ABAP programming language
HANA data modeling — learning about HANA's data modeling capabilities, including support for star and snowflake schema designs

knowledge.what_it_is: EMC Documentum (DMS) is an enterprise content management platform that enables organizations to manage, store, and retrieve unstructured data. It provides features such as document management, workflow automation, and search functionality.
what_you_will_learn: Documentum Content Server — the core repository for storing and managing documents, with features like versioning, check-in/check-out, and metadata management
Documentum xPlore — a web-based interface for searching, browsing, and retrieving documents, with features like faceted search and content analytics
Documentum Workflow — a business process automation tool that enables organizations to model, execute, and monitor workflows across the enterprise
Documentum Federation Services — a feature that allows integration with other systems and repositories through APIs and web services
EMC Documentum Content Server architecture — understanding the components, layers, and interactions within the platform, including the repository, metadata management, and search functionality
Documentum security and access control — configuring user roles, permissions, and authentication mechanisms to ensure data integrity and compliance
EMC Documentum migration strategies — planning and executing migrations from legacy systems or other content management platforms to EMC DMS

knowledge.what_it_is: IBM Business Process Manager (BPM) is a comprehensive business process management platform that enables organizations to model, execute, and monitor business processes across multiple systems and applications. It provides a unified platform for process modeling, execution, and monitoring, allowing businesses to improve efficiency, reduce costs, and enhance customer experience.
what_you_will_learn: Process Modeling — creating and managing business processes using BPMN 2.0 notation, including gateways, events, and tasks
Process Execution — deploying and executing business processes on IBM BPM runtime engine, including process instance management and monitoring
Human Task Management — assigning human tasks to users, including task assignment, escalation, and completion tracking
Integration with External Systems — integrating IBM BPM with external systems using web services, APIs, and messaging protocols (e.g., JMS, MQ)
Business Rules Management — defining and managing business rules using IBM Operational Decision Manager (ODM), including rule authoring, testing, and deployment
Process Monitoring and Analytics — monitoring process performance using IBM BPM's built-in analytics capabilities, including KPI tracking and reporting
Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA) Integration — integrating IBM BPM with SOA-based systems using web services and APIs

knowledge.what_it_is: Salesforce is a cloud-based enterprise software as a service (SaaS) platform that provides customer relationship management (CRM) and enterprise resource planning (ERP) capabilities. As a Software Architect, you will design and implement scalable, secure, and efficient solutions on the Salesforce platform.
what_you_will_learn: Salesforce Architecture Patterns — designing scalable, modular, and maintainable architectures using patterns like Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA), Event-Driven Architecture (EDA), and Microservices Architecture
Apex Programming Language — writing custom business logic in Apex to extend Salesforce functionality, including triggers, classes, and interfaces
Visualforce Pages — creating custom user interfaces for Salesforce applications using Visualforce markup language, including page controllers, components, and layouts
Salesforce APIs (SOAP, REST) — integrating external systems with Salesforce using SOAP and REST APIs, including authentication, authorization, and data exchange
Salesforce Security Model — designing secure solutions that adhere to Salesforce security best practices, including user permissions, access control, and data encryption
Salesforce Integration Patterns — implementing integration patterns like Hub-and-Spoke, Star, and Point-to-Point using tools like MuleSoft, Jitterbit, or Informatica PowerCenter
Salesforce Analytics (Tableau, Einstein Analytics) — designing and implementing data visualizations and analytics solutions using Tableau or Einstein Analytics
Salesforce Mobile Development (Mobile SDK) — building mobile applications that integrate with Salesforce using the Mobile SDK for iOS and Android
subtopics: mobile sdk for ios and android

knowledge.what_it_is: A trio of popular JavaScript frameworks used for building complex frontend applications, each with its own strengths and weaknesses.
what_you_will_learn: Monolithic architecture vs. Microfrontends — understanding the trade-offs between a single, monolithic application and breaking it down into smaller, independent pieces
Component-based architecture — designing reusable UI components that can be easily composed together to build complex applications
State management with Redux, MobX, or React Context API — choosing the right approach for managing global state in a large-scale application
Server-side rendering (SSR) and static site generation (SSG) with Next.js, Nuxt.js, or Angular Universal — optimizing performance by pre-rendering content on the server
Code splitting and lazy loading with React.lazy(), Vue's async components, or Angular's dynamic imports — improving application startup time and reducing bundle size
Type safety with TypeScript, Flow, or Babel's type annotations — ensuring code quality and catch errors early in development
Dependency injection with Angular's Dependency Injection system, Vue's inject function, or React's Context API — decoupling components from dependencies and making them more testable

knowledge.what_it_is: Single-Page Applications (SPAs), Server-Side Rendering (SSR), and Static Site Generation (SSG) are frontend architecture techniques used to optimize user experience, improve SEO, and enhance performance. As a Software Architect, you'll need to understand the trade-offs between these approaches and choose the best fit for your project.
what_you_will_learn: Single-Page Application (SPA) — a client-side rendered application that updates dynamically without full page reloads
Server-Side Rendering (SSR) — rendering HTML on the server, sending it to the client, and updating the DOM client-side for improved SEO and faster initial load times
Static Site Generation (SSG) — pre-rendering HTML at build time, serving static files from a CDN for ultra-fast page loads and zero maintenance
Client-Side Routing — using libraries like React Router or Vue Router to manage client-side routing and navigation
Server-Side Rendering with Node.js — using frameworks like Express.js or Next.js to render server-side rendered pages with Node.js
Static Site Generators like Gatsby, Hugo, or Jekyll — choosing the right tool for SSG and integrating it with your frontend framework of choice
Caching Strategies — implementing caching mechanisms like Redis or Memcached to reduce server load and improve performance
Content Delivery Networks (CDNs) — using CDNs like Cloudflare or AWS CloudFront to distribute static assets globally and reduce latency

knowledge.what_it_is: Microfrontends are an architectural pattern that enables multiple independent frontend applications to be composed together into a single, cohesive user experience. This approach allows for greater flexibility, scalability, and maintainability of large-scale web applications.
what_you_will_learn: Monolithic architecture vs microfrontend architecture — understanding the trade-offs between a single, monolithic application and multiple, independent frontend applications
Frontend modules — defining self-contained pieces of functionality that can be composed together to form a larger application
Module federation — using Webpack Module Federation or other tools to enable communication and data sharing between microfrontends
API composition — designing APIs that allow microfrontends to interact with each other and with backend services
Service discovery — implementing mechanisms for microfrontends to discover and communicate with each other at runtime
State management in microfrontends — understanding how to manage state across multiple, independent applications
Security considerations for microfrontends — ensuring that data is properly secured when shared between microfrontends

knowledge.what_it_is: The World Wide Web Consortium (W3C) and WHATWG are two organizations that develop and maintain web standards, ensuring interoperability and consistency across browsers. W3C focuses on HTML, CSS, and other technologies, while WHATWG works on HTML5, Web Storage, and other related specifications.
what_you_will_learn: HTML5 specification — understanding the latest features, including multimedia, canvas, and offline storage
WHATWG vs W3C: differences in approach, governance, and scope of work for web standards development
Web Components — a suite of technologies (Shadow DOM, Custom Elements, HTML Imports) enabling reusable UI components
HTML5 semantic elements — using header, footer, nav, section, article, aside, figure, figcaption to improve accessibility and SEO
CSS Grid Layout — a 2D layout system for flexible grid-based design, replacing floats and flexbox in some cases
Responsive Web Design (RWD) principles — applying media queries, viewport units, and other techniques for adaptive layouts
Web Storage API — using localStorage and sessionStorage to store data locally on the client-side
Service Workers — enabling offline caching, push notifications, and background sync with modern web APIs

knowledge.what_it_is: Large Scale Scrum (LeSS) is an iterative and incremental software development framework that scales Scrum to larger teams and projects, while maintaining its core principles of flexibility, adaptability, and continuous improvement. It was created by Craig Larman and Bas Vodde in 2005.
what_you_will_learn: Scrum of Scrums — a scaled version of the daily Scrum meeting for large teams, where team leads or representatives discuss progress and issues
Feature Teams — cross-functional teams that work on specific features or user stories, promoting collaboration and self-organization
Component Teams — smaller teams within feature teams that focus on specific components or sub-systems, improving modularity and maintainability
Value Stream Mapping (VSM) — a visualization technique to identify and eliminate waste in the software development process, optimizing flow and value delivery
Innovation Accounting — a framework for measuring and improving innovation, using metrics such as Lead Time, Throughput, and Cycle Time
LeSS Framework Components — an overview of the key components that make up the LeSS framework, including Scrum of Scrums, Feature Teams, Component Teams, and Innovation Accounting
Scaling Scrum with LeSS Principles — applying the core principles of LeSS to scale Scrum in large teams and projects, such as flexibility, adaptability, and continuous improvement

knowledge.what_it_is: SaFE (Scaled Agile Framework Enterprise) is an extension of the Scaled Agile Framework (SAFe), designed specifically for large enterprises, providing a structured approach to implementing Lean-Agile principles at scale. It emphasizes collaboration, continuous improvement, and delivery of value to customers.
what_you_will_learn: Value Streams — identifying and optimizing end-to-end flows of value through the organization
PIs (Program Increments) — planning and executing large-scale development iterations
ARTs (Agile Release Trains) — teams working together to deliver a Program Increment, with a focus on continuous integration and delivery
Solution Train — a framework for implementing SaFE in large enterprises, emphasizing collaboration and alignment across multiple teams
Portfolio Management — prioritizing and managing the flow of value through the organization, using the Portfolio Kanban board
Value Stream Mapping — visualizing and optimizing end-to-end processes to improve efficiency and effectiveness
Lean-Agile Leadership — developing skills for leading and facilitating Lean-Agile transformations in large enterprises

knowledge.what_it_is: Extreme Programming (XP) is an agile software development methodology that emphasizes technical practices and collaboration between developers, customers, and stakeholders to deliver high-quality software quickly and efficiently. It was first introduced by Kent Beck in 1999 and has since become a widely adopted approach in the industry.
what_you_will_learn: Pair Programming — working in pairs to improve code quality, reduce errors, and increase knowledge sharing
Test-Driven Development (TDD) — writing automated tests before coding to ensure software meets requirements and is maintainable
Continuous Integration (CI) — integrating code changes into a central repository frequently to catch defects early and improve collaboration
Refactoring — regularly improving existing code to make it more maintainable, efficient, and easier to understand
Collective Code Ownership — all team members are responsible for the entire codebase, promoting knowledge sharing and reducing technical debt
Continuous Improvement (Kaizen) — regularly reflecting on processes and practices to identify areas for improvement and implement changes
Customer Involvement — involving customers in the development process through regular feedback and iteration to ensure software meets their needs
Sustainable Pace — maintaining a sustainable workload and avoiding burnout by prioritizing tasks, managing dependencies, and taking breaks

knowledge.what_it_is: Kanban is an iterative delivery method that focuses on visualizing work, limiting work-in-progress (WIP), and continuous improvement. It originated from lean manufacturing principles and has been widely adopted in software development to improve team efficiency, reduce lead time, and enhance collaboration.
what_you_will_learn: Kanban board — a visual representation of workflow stages, used for tracking work items and identifying bottlenecks
WIP limits — setting constraints on the number of tasks allowed in each stage to prevent overloading and improve flow
Service-level agreements (SLAs) — defining service quality expectations and response times to meet customer needs
Lead time — measuring the time it takes for a task to move from start to finish, including all stages and approvals
Cycle time — calculating the average time spent on each stage of the workflow, excluding lead time
Throughput — determining the number of tasks completed within a given timeframe, used for capacity planning
Kanban metrics — tracking key performance indicators (KPIs) such as cycle time, throughput, and lead time to inform process improvements
Continuous integration and continuous deployment (CI/CD) pipelines — integrating Kanban with automated testing and deployment processes

knowledge.what_it_is: Scrum is an iterative and incremental Agile framework used by software development teams to manage and complete complex projects using a structured methodology, emphasizing teamwork, accountability, and iterative progress toward well-defined goals.
what_you_will_learn: Roles — understanding the Scrum roles: Product Owner, Scrum Master, Development Team, and their responsibilities
Sprint Planning — planning and executing sprints with clear objectives, scope, and timelines
Product Backlog Refinement — refining and prioritizing product backlog items for efficient development
Daily Stand-ups — conducting daily meetings to discuss progress, impediments, and plans
Sprint Review — reviewing and demonstrating sprint outcomes to stakeholders and team members
Sprint Retrospective — reflecting on sprint performance, identifying improvements, and implementing changes
Scrum Board — using a visual board for tracking and managing work items across the development process
Kanban Integration — integrating Scrum with Kanban principles for continuous improvement and flow-based development

knowledge.what_it_is: Project Management Institute (PMI) is a globally recognized organization that provides standards, guidelines, and certifications for project management professionals. As a Software Architect, understanding PMI's framework and methodologies is crucial for managing complex software development projects.
what_you_will_learn: Agile Methodologies — applying Agile principles to software development, including Scrum, Kanban, and Lean
Waterfall Model — understanding the traditional, linear approach to project management in software development
Iterative Development — breaking down large projects into smaller iterations with incremental delivery
Incremental Build-up — building a system incrementally by adding new features or components
Phased Approach — dividing a project into distinct phases with clear goals and deliverables
Project Life Cycle — understanding the stages of a software development project, including initiation, planning, execution, monitoring & control, and closure
Earned Value Management (EVM) — measuring project performance using metrics such as Cost Variance (CV), Schedule Variance (SV), and Performance Index (PI)
Critical Path Method (CPM) — identifying the critical tasks that determine a project's overall duration and resource allocation

knowledge.what_it_is: ITIL (Information Technology Infrastructure Library) is a widely adopted framework for IT service management, developed by AXELOS. It provides a structured approach to managing IT services and ensuring they align with business objectives.
what_you_will_learn: Service Strategy — understanding the business case for IT services, identifying opportunities for improvement
Service Design — designing IT services that meet business needs, including service catalogs and service level agreements (SLAs)
Service Transition — planning and implementing changes to IT services, including change management and release management
Service Operation — managing day-to-day IT service operations, including incident management and problem management
Continual Service Improvement — identifying opportunities for improving IT services, including service reporting and metrics
ITIL Processes — understanding the 26 core processes of ITIL, including service desk, change control, and capacity management
Service Level Management (SLM) — defining and managing SLAs with customers, including key performance indicators (KPIs)
Capacity Management — ensuring that IT services have sufficient capacity to meet business needs

knowledge.what_it_is: RUP (Rational Unified Process) is an iterative and incremental software development process framework, developed by Rational Software (now part of IBM). It emphasizes a structured approach to software development, with a focus on delivering working software at regular intervals. RUP is widely used in enterprise software development for its flexibility and adaptability.
what_you_will_learn: Iterative Development — breaking down the project into smaller iterations, each with its own set of requirements and deliverables
Incremental Development — building and delivering working software incrementally, with continuous improvement and refinement
Use Cases — identifying and documenting functional requirements as use cases, including preconditions, postconditions, and main success scenario
Activity Diagrams — modeling the dynamic behavior of a system using activity diagrams, which show the sequence of activities that occur in a particular situation
State Machine Diagrams — modeling the life cycle of an object or a system using state machine diagrams, which show the different states an object can be in and how it transitions between them
Deployment Planning — planning and executing the deployment of software to production environments, including creating deployment plans, scripts, and procedures
Project Management — managing RUP projects using tools like IBM Rational ClearCase, IBM Rational ClearQuest, or other project management software

knowledge.what_it_is: BABOK (Business Analysis Body of Knowledge) is a framework developed by IIBA (International Institute of Business Analysis) to guide business analysts in their work. It provides a structured approach to business analysis, including techniques for requirements gathering, analysis, and documentation.
what_you_will_learn: Business Analysis Framework — understanding the BABOK framework's nine knowledge areas: planning, initiating, analyzing, specifying, elaborating, designing, constructing, testing, and delivering
Business Process Modeling Notation (BPMN) — creating visual representations of business processes using BPMN 2.0 standard
Use Cases — defining and documenting functional requirements from the user's perspective
Requirements Elicitation Techniques — applying techniques like interviews, surveys, workshops, and observation to gather requirements
Business Requirements Document (BRD) — creating a document that captures business requirements, including scope, assumptions, and constraints
Data Flow Diagrams (DFDs) — visualizing data flows between processes and entities in an organization
Entity-Relationship Diagrams (ERDs) — modeling the relationships between entities in a database or information system

knowledge.what_it_is: PRINCE2 (Projects IN Controlled Environments) is a widely used project management methodology developed by AXELOS, focusing on delivering projects within time, cost, and quality constraints. It's an iterative approach that emphasizes flexibility and adaptability.
what_you_will_learn: Project lifecycle — understanding the seven processes: Initiating, Planning, Controlling, Monitoring & Controlling, Stage Gates, Managing Product Delivery
Business Case — developing a clear justification for the project, including costs, benefits, and risks
Organization Structure — defining roles and responsibilities within the project team, including the Project Manager, Senior Responsible Owner (SRO), and Project Assurance
Quality Management — ensuring that all aspects of the project meet the required standards, including quality plans, checklists, and reviews
Risk Management — identifying, assessing, and mitigating risks throughout the project lifecycle
Configuration Management — controlling changes to the project's products, including documentation, code, and deliverables
Change Control — managing changes to the project scope, schedule, or budget through a formal change control process
Lessons Learned — documenting and reviewing project experiences to improve future projects

knowledge.what_it_is: IAF (Integrated Architecture Framework) is an open-standard, vendor-neutral framework for developing and implementing enterprise architecture, enabling organizations to create a unified view of their IT landscape. It provides a structured approach to architecting solutions that meet business needs.
what_you_will_learn: Enterprise architecture principles — understanding the fundamental concepts of IAF, including separation of concerns, abstraction, and modularity
Architecture patterns — learning about common patterns used in enterprise architecture, such as Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA), Microservices Architecture, and Event-Driven Architecture (EDA)
Service-oriented design — designing services that are loosely coupled, highly cohesive, and easily composable
API design principles — understanding how to design APIs that are RESTful, secure, and scalable
Microservices architecture patterns — learning about containerization, service discovery, and load balancing in microservices environments
Event-driven architecture (EDA) fundamentals — understanding the concepts of events, event producers, and event consumers
Service composition and orchestration — learning how to compose services into larger applications and orchestrate their interactions
Security and compliance considerations — understanding how to design secure and compliant enterprise architectures

knowledge.what_it_is: TOGAF (The Open Group Architecture Framework) is an enterprise architecture framework used by software architects to design, implement, and maintain large-scale IT systems. It provides a comprehensive approach to architecting business solutions, focusing on integration, standardization, and reuse.
what_you_will_learn: Architecture Development Method (ADM) — the core process of TOGAF, consisting of nine phases: Architecture Vision, Business Case, Architecture Design, Implementation, Release, Service Integration, Service Migration, Retirement, and Disposal

Architecture Content Framework (ACF) — a set of building blocks used to create an enterprise architecture, including business architecture, data architecture, application architecture, technology architecture, and security architecture
Enterprise Continuum (EC) — a repository of reusable architectural patterns, frameworks, and standards that can be applied across the organization
Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA) — a design pattern used in TOGAF to create loosely coupled systems, promoting reuse and flexibility
Zachman Framework — a framework for classifying and organizing enterprise architecture artifacts, including data, applications, technology, and security
TOGAF 9.2 Content Pack — an extension of the TOGAF framework that includes additional content and guidance for implementing enterprise architecture in various industries and domains

knowledge.what_it_is: Business Process Management (BPM) and Business Process Execution Language (BPEL) are industry-standard technologies used by software architects to model, execute, and monitor business processes. BPM enables organizations to streamline their operations, improve efficiency, and reduce costs.
what_you_will_learn: BPMN — the standard language for modeling business processes, including activities, gateways, events, and artifacts
BPEL — a process definition language used to execute business processes across multiple systems and organizations
Process Orchestration — coordinating multiple processes and services to achieve complex business outcomes
Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA) — designing business processes around reusable services
Event-Driven Architecture (EDA) — using events to trigger process execution and improve real-time decision-making
Business Rules Engine (BRE) — implementing business logic as rules-based decisions within the BPM system
Process Monitoring and Analytics — tracking key performance indicators (KPIs), identifying bottlenecks, and optimizing process flows

knowledge.what_it_is: Git is a free and open-source distributed version control system that allows developers to track changes made to code, collaborate with others, and manage different versions of their project. As a Software Architect, you will use Git to manage large-scale software development projects, ensure reproducibility, and maintain a clean and organized codebase.
what_you_will_learn: Git repositories — creating and managing central hubs for version control
Branching strategies — using feature branches, release branches, and hotfix branches to manage parallel development
Merge requests — integrating changes from one branch into another, with options for squash, merge, and rebase
Conflict resolution — handling merge conflicts using Git's built-in tools and third-party plugins like Resolving Conflicts
Git tags — creating and managing version labels, including annotated tags and lightweight tags
Git hooks — customizing the commit process with pre-commit, post-commit, and other hooks
Submodules — incorporating external projects into your repository using Git submodules
Git stash — temporarily storing changes to be applied later, useful for switching between feature branches

knowledge.what_it_is: Slack is a cloud-based communication platform designed for teams, developed by Slack Technologies (now part of Salesforce). It provides real-time messaging, video conferencing, and file sharing features. As a Software Architect, you will use Slack to facilitate team collaboration, integrate it with other tools and services, and ensure seamless communication across the organization.
what_you_will_learn: Slack Channels — organizing conversations by topic or project, with customizable permissions and notifications
Workflows — automating repetitive tasks using custom-built workflows in Slack's platform
Integrations — connecting Slack to other tools and services via APIs, such as GitHub, Trello, or Google Drive
Custom Apps — building custom applications on top of Slack's API to provide tailored solutions for the team
Slack Bots — creating automated bots using Slack's Bot Framework to perform tasks or answer questions
Permissions and Roles — managing user permissions and roles within Slack to ensure secure collaboration
Single Sign-On (SSO) Integration — integrating Slack with existing SSO systems to simplify user authentication

knowledge.what_it_is: Trello is a visual project management tool that uses Kanban boards to organize tasks and workflows, popular among software architects due to its flexibility and ease of use.
what_you_will_learn: Kanban boards — creating digital boards for visualizing workflow stages and task assignments
Lists and cards — organizing tasks into lists (e.g., To-Do, In Progress, Done) and using cards for detailed task descriptions
Labels and colors — categorizing tasks with labels and colors to facilitate filtering and prioritization
Board permissions — setting up access controls for team members and stakeholders to manage workflow visibility and collaboration
Integrations with other tools — connecting Trello with external services like GitHub, Slack, or Google Drive for seamless data exchange
Custom fields and workflows — extending Trello's functionality using custom fields and automating workflows with Butler or Zapier integrations

knowledge.what_it_is: Atlassian tools are a suite of software collaboration and development tools, including Jira, Confluence, Bitbucket, Trello, and others. As a Software Architect, you will use these tools to plan, track, and deliver software projects.
what_you_will_learn: Jira — project management tool for tracking issues, bugs, and features; understanding workflows, boards, and reporting
Confluence — wiki-based documentation tool for collaborative knowledge sharing and content creation; using spaces, pages, and macros
Bitbucket — source code management tool for version control and collaboration on Git repositories; understanding branches, pull requests, and code reviews
Trello — agile project management tool for visualizing workflows and tracking tasks; using boards, lists, and cards for Kanban-style development
Jira Software — planning and tracking tool for software development teams; understanding sprints, backlogs, and velocity metrics
Atlassian Marketplace — ecosystem of third-party integrations and plugins for extending Atlassian tools' functionality; exploring available add-ons and APIs
Atlassian API — RESTful API for accessing and manipulating data across Atlassian tools; using OAuth authentication and API keys for integration
